business_reasoning: Sequential identifier; encodes no predictive signal and would leak row ordering into tree splits.
Operationally invalid, but we do NOT automatically remove them. Flag with is_zero_adults=1 and let the model learn their pattern. Revisit if model audit shows noise.

decision: Keep zero-price rows (n=545) for cancellation model
business_reasoning: May represent complimentary stays. Add price_is_zero flag so the model can distinguish them from paid bookings. Log-transform will handle the rest.

decision: Exclude zero-price rows from price regression model
business_reasoning: Predicting a $0 price has no operational meaning. These rows corrupt the regression target.
High-end prices are real. Removing them would bias the model toward economy bookings. Apply log1p transform instead — compresses the tail without information loss.
Possibly day-use or same-arrival/departure records. Flag with is_zero_nights=1. Investigate further before dropping.
Long-advance bookings are genuine (events, weddings). Apply log1p transform.
Dates like Feb 30 cannot exist. Drop or correct after manual inspection.
recommendation: DROP after validation — count TBD in this run.

business_reasoning: Only 3 rows; no material impact. Keep to avoid artificial filtering.

decision: Rare category handling (Room_Type 3 n=7, Meal Plan 3 n=5)
business_reasoning: One-hot encoding would create near-zero-variance columns. Use target encoding computed on training fold only — never on validation/test.
32.8% cancellation rate is a moderate imbalance. Stratified splits preserve the ratio. Evaluate class-weighted loss or SMOTE only after baseline.
